turns.text: Hello Camellia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a strange thing, isn’t it? To spend twenty years in a lab in Boston treating life like a series of variables to be controlled and then suddenly find yourself as the variable. I’m forty-four. I know the stats—the steep drop-off in egg quality, the chromosomal risks, the "advanced maternal age" warnings that get stamped on my chart like a scarlet letter. 

My doctor, she’s perfectly lovely, but there’s a gap between us. When I ask her about the potential epigenetic consequences of my cortisol levels—which, let’s be honest, have been sky-high just from the stress of this decision—she just gives me this warm, placating smile and says, "Let's focus on the basics." She’s trying to be kind, I know. She wants me to breathe. But my brain doesn't just "breathe." It thinks about organogenesis and oxidative stress. I’ve spent my life studying how the womb is the first environment, and now I’m terrified that my own body might not be the pristine, steady harbor I need it to be. 

I’m not a "late bloomer." I’m just a woman who finally felt like she had the internal infrastructure to hold someone else. But now that I’m here, the science feels like a heavy coat I can’t take off. It’s hard to just be a patient.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I expected a partner in the process—someone I could hold a real, intellectual conversation with. Because I spend my days reviewing longitudinal studies and thinking about prenatal programming, I imagined my preconception care would be a collaborative, data-driven experience. I pictured us mapping out a biological game plan, maybe discussing the latest in nutrigenomics or the specific metabolic markers I should be mindful of given my age. 

But the reality? It’s been much more reductive. The appointments feel like a rapid-fire checklist. When I walk in, I see the doctor glance at my file, see "44," see "single," and I can almost watch her switch into a simplified mode of communication. It’s like she’s trying to manage my anxiety rather than engage my intellect. 

When I brought up the research regarding maternal stress and long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes, she just patted my hand and told me to "try to relax." It felt dismissive, even if she meant it as comfort. I want to feel like a collaborator, but instead, I feel like a biological outlier being managed through a standardized filter. It’s isolating. I’m seeking a bridge between my professional understanding of biology and the reality of my own aging body, but I’m finding that the system really only knows how to categorize me, not listen to me.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise, and I suppose the most painful one, was how quickly my agency vanished the moment I crossed that threshold. I walked in there with folders, with specific questions about my own health, expecting to be treated like an intelligent participant in my own care. I thought, *“I understand the biology, so we can have a high-level conversation about how to optimize this.”*

Instead, the surprise was how quickly I was reduced to a "geriatric pregnancy" statistic. It’s like the moment that label gets applied, the healthcare provider stops looking at *me*—the woman who has spent decades studying how cells grow and adapt—and starts looking at a set of risks that need to be mitigated.

It felt like I was suddenly an object to be observed rather than a person to be heard. I expected a conversation about metabolic optimization, but I got a "don't-worry-your-pretty-little-head-about-the-science" kind of reaction. I didn't realize how lonely that would be. I thought being an informed patient would make the experience smoother, but it actually made the disconnect feel sharper. I went in looking for a specialist who would validate my knowledge, but I found someone who just wanted to keep me "compliant and calm." It’s an incredibly frustrating, almost infantilizing, shift in power.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I spent years telling myself that emotional readiness was about stability. I built a career, a home, and a life that felt—on paper—like a fortress. I thought that by the time I was ready to do this, I would be entirely anchored. I expected to feel like a calm, steady sea for a child to sail into.

The reality? It’s completely different. It turns out that having the "intellectual infrastructure" doesn’t stop the phantom fears from gnawing at you at three in the morning. I thought I’d be serene. Instead, I feel fragile. It’s like everything I know about the biology of development has turned into a weapon I’m using against myself. I look at my own body and I don’t see a "safe harbor" anymore; I see a series of risks I need to account for.

I’m mourning the version of myself that thought she could out-think or out-plan nature. The expectation was that my experience as a scientist would empower me, but it has actually made the weight of this responsibility feel almost physical. I’m not just worried about the pregnancy; I’m terrified of the "what if" because I know exactly what the literature says about every little deviation. I am steadier than I was at thirty, yes, but I am also far more aware of how precious—and precarious—this all is.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

It’s a wasteland, honestly. I went into this expecting the digital landscape to be a mix of rigorous, evidence-based guidance and supportive, community-driven insights. I thought I’d find tools that allowed me to input my own data—my cycles, my metabolic markers, my research-heavy concerns—and get back something personalized.

What I actually found was a deluge of "baby-speak" and aggressive marketing. Everything online is either curated for a twenty-five-year-old who just wants a cute announcement photo, or it’s fear-mongering garbage designed to sell supplements to women my age who are feeling vulnerable. The apps are worse; they’re patronizing. They tell me to "drink water" and "stay positive," which feels almost insulting given the complexities I’m staring at. 

I’m looking for a nuanced discussion about biological aging, but the internet wants to give me generic platitudes or terrifying, unvetted anecdotes. There is no middle ground. It’s either "you’re too old, good luck" or "don't stress, it’ll happen!" Neither is useful. I wanted information that respects the science I’ve dedicated my life to, but instead, I’m treated like I’m just starting to learn how human reproduction works. It makes me feel incredibly isolated, like I’m navigating a map that’s drawn in crayon while I’m trying to read a textbook.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The gap is almost dizzying. On the one hand, the digital world—the blogs, the forums, the wellness influencers—constantly pushes this narrative of "optimizing" everything. They suggest that if you just buy the right supplements, track the right hormones with a $300 device, and eat the perfect anti-inflammatory diet, you can essentially "hack" your age. It creates this false sense of control, telling me that I can override the biological reality of being forty-four if I’m just diligent enough.

Then I go to my doctor, and she swings the pendulum entirely to the other side. She’s so focused on minimizing risks and managing the "advanced maternal age" label that she dismisses everything. She treats the desire for granular detail as a symptom of anxiety, rather than a symptom of being a researcher. 

The digital world says, "You can control your future with this bottle of pills." My doctor says, "Don't bother looking at the data, just go home and try." Neither of them is actually meeting me where I am. I’m trapped between a fantasy of total control and a reality of clinical dismissal. No one is telling me, "Here is the biological truth of your situation, here are the limitations, and here is how we walk that path together with intelligence." I’m left to synthesize it all myself, which is exactly what I’m trying to avoid doing.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic, diagnosed illness in the traditional sense, but I have a "condition" that the system treats as a pathology: my age. In my field, we talk about "maternal programming"—the way a mother’s physiology shapes the fetus—so I came to my doctor expecting a comprehensive, proactive assessment of my baseline. I wanted a discussion about my metabolic health, my specific nutrient absorption, and perhaps a deep dive into my personal environmental stressors. I wanted a plan that felt as precise as the research I conduct daily.

Instead, when I bring up my history or ask for a more rigorous, data-informed approach, I get met with a wall of generic protocols. The system isn't set up to "prepare" a woman of forty-four; it’s set up to "monitor" her for failure. 

I expected an advocate who would help me optimize my body to be the best possible environment for a baby. What I got was someone who treats my desire for information as a nuisance. It’s like they’re waiting for me to hit a complication so they can start their algorithm. They aren't interested in the "pre" part of preconception; they’re just waiting for the "conception" to happen so they can start the risk management. It makes me feel like I’m being managed by a machine that isn't programmed to handle someone who knows how the machine works.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Proactive? Not even remotely. The system isn't designed to reach out; it's designed to process. I am the one doing the heavy lifting, essentially managing my own care because the standard of care feels like it’s built for someone who doesn’t ask questions.

If I didn't advocate for myself—if I didn't push for specific blood panels or demand we talk about the implications of my age—I’m convinced I’d just be shuffled through the door with a prenatal vitamin recommendation and a "good luck." I find myself having to adopt this performative "calm, compliant patient" persona just to get them to listen to me for an extra three minutes. If I show up with my research, I’m "that" patient—the one who’s too educated, too intense, too high-maintenance. 

It’s exhausting to have to constantly prove that my curiosity comes from a place of professional rigor and not just "neurotic googling." I’m not asking for extra attention because I’m dramatic; I’m asking for it because I know the biological stakes. Every time I have to fight to be taken seriously, it spikes my cortisol, which is exactly the thing I’m trying to manage. It’s a vicious, ironic cycle. I shouldn’t have to be my own doctor, but in this system, silence is seen as agreement, and I refuse to be silent.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

It’s a complicated question, given that I’m navigating this as a woman who never married. My "partner" in this isn’t a spouse, but rather an intentional, chosen connection—someone who is supportive but, let’s be honest, fundamentally removed from the biological weight of it all.

Before I started this, I imagined that "partnership" would mean we were both equally invested in the science of it. I pictured us sitting down together to look at the health metrics or discussing the implications of our combined genetic history. I expected him to be as burdened by the "what-ifs" as I am. I wanted that shared mantle.

In reality, his involvement feels… lighter. And I don’t mean that as a critique, but as a painful observation of our different realities. He is supportive, he asks how I’m feeling, and he’s ready to be a father. But he cannot—and does not—feel the visceral, 24/7 internal clock ticking in his own cells. He sees the "plan" as a series of positive steps toward a goal, while I see it as a high-stakes, microscopic orchestration where I am the sole theater of operations. When I express my fears, he looks at me with love, but he’s looking at me from the outside. The weight of the programming is mine to carry. It’s a lonely kind of partnership.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

The burden hasn’t just been disproportionate; it has been absolute. Every single day, I am hyper-aware that my body is the only landscape where this potential life exists. Whether it’s the folic acid timing, the avoidance of endocrine disruptors in my own apartment, or the constant, silent monitoring of my own stress-induced physiological responses—it’s all me. 

What drives this? It’s twofold. First, it’s the biology. You can’t outsource the womb. Even if I had a partner who was the most dedicated man on earth, he doesn’t have the biological skin in the game that I do. He’s not the one who has to worry about egg quality or the microscopic environment of the uterus. 

But secondly, it’s the systemic failure I keep bumping into. The healthcare system, at least in my experience, doesn’t talk to the "village." They talk to the patient. Everything is directed at me: my diet, my lifestyle, my history, my age. They don't engage with the collaborative reality of conception. It’s as if the medical establishment views reproduction as a solitary, female-centric project, which only deepens the feeling that I am the sole architect of this child’s future. It turns a profound life event into a solitary lab experiment where I am both the scientist and the subject, and frankly, that’s a very heavy mantle to wear alone.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't experienced a pregnancy loss, but that exact "what if" is exactly what keeps me up at night. Because I work in research, I’m intimately familiar with the statistics—the rising probability of miscarriage as the years tick by—and I’ve seen the data on how clinical environments often handle that.

If I were to walk back in there after a loss, I expect the system would handle me with a cold, efficient sort of sympathy—the kind that prioritizes the "next steps" and "recovery protocols" while leaving the emotional excavation entirely to me. I fear that the care would be purely reactive, centered on whether I’m physically "cleared" to try again, rather than addressing the trauma of the loss or the fear of a repeat.

My experience so far has taught me that the medical system views these things through a lens of clinical cycles. They don't see the grief or the anxiety as part of the biological picture; they see it as a sidebar, something for a therapist, not a doctor. I suspect that if I lost a pregnancy, I would be left to navigate that silence on my own, just as I’ve had to navigate the frustration of being a "geriatric" patient. The support I’d *need*—someone to help me understand the "why" and process the weight of the "what now"—doesn't feel like something the current system is equipped to offer.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

I think there’s a slight misunderstanding—I haven't experienced a loss yet, but that’s precisely what makes the *anticipation* of one feel so sharp. It’s like living on a fault line. Because I spend my days analyzing data on prenatal development, I am constantly calculating the probability of a "negative outcome," as the literature would coldly put it. 

The issue is that when I try to talk about that fear—when I try to voice the very real possibility of loss given my age—the system just shuts it down. They equate "risk awareness" with "pathological anxiety." I haven't even conceived yet, but I already feel like if I were to suffer a loss, the clinical response would be to simply look at my chart, shrug at my age, and say, "Well, the odds were against you from the start." 

There is no room in the current model for the *humanity* of the loss. If I were to lose a pregnancy, I fear the healthcare system would treat it like a failed experiment rather than a profound, transformative grief. It feels like they are waiting to write me off as a "failed cycle" before I’ve even had the chance to try. That dismissal of my emotional reality feels just as dangerous as the medical risks themselves.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I suppose if there’s one thing I want to impress upon you—and through you, those who design these care systems—it’s the difference between being "cared for" and being "managed."

There is a profound, aching distance between the clinical protocols the system demands and the actual, living human experience of trying to bring life into the world at forty-four. I am not a risk factor. I am not a collection of cells at the end of their reproductive timeline. I am a woman who has spent her life understanding the fragility of our beginnings, and that knowledge is both my greatest asset and my deepest source of terror.

We need a maternity care model that stops treating the *patient’s* intelligence as a hurdle. I don't want to be placated with hand-patting and empty platitudes about "relaxing." I want a partner who can hold the complexity with me—someone who acknowledges that yes, the biology is daunting, and yes, the risks are real, but who can navigate that truth without treating me like a broken system. We are more than our charts. If the medical community could just see that, it would change everything. It would turn a cold, lonely marathon into a journey that actually feels human. Thank you for listening; it’s the first time in this entire process that someone hasn't told me to just "breathe and let it go."
